title: Cat Tremors and Wobbly Walking: Emergency Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat shaking or walking off-balance? Learn why tremors and wobbly walking are serious symptoms and when to seek emergency veterinary care.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden tremors and wobbly walking in cats are medical emergencies that require immediate veterinary care, as they often indicate serious neurological issues or toxic ingestion.

What does it mean if my cat is shaking or walking like they are drunk?

Tremors are involuntary, rhythmic muscle contractions that may look like shaking or shivering. Wobbly walking, medically known as ataxia, refers to a lack of coordination that makes your cat appear 'drunk' or off-balance. Together, these symptoms usually indicate a disruption in the nervous system, brain, or inner ear.

Should I take my cat to the emergency vet for tremors and uncoordinated walking?

  • The urgency level for these symptoms is High; sudden onset tremors and uncoordinated movement are considered medical emergencies.
  • These signs can be caused by life-threatening issues such as toxin ingestion, neurological trauma, severe metabolic imbalances, or infectious diseases.
  • You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if you notice these changes in your cat.

How can I help my veterinarian diagnose my cat's tremors and wobbling?

  • Capture a 10-to-30-second video of the behavior if it is safe to do so, as neurological symptoms can be intermittent.
  • Provide the video to your veterinarian to offer invaluable diagnostic clues that may not be visible by the time you reach the clinic.
  • Allow the medical team to see the specific type of tremor or how your cat misplaces their paws to help determine the severity and the likely location of the problem within the nervous system.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with tremors and wobbly walking, emergency triage should consider several potential etiologies. Demyelinating disorders can manifest as gross whole-body tremors involving the limbs, trunk, head, and eyes, lessening during rest but exacerbated by arousal or excitement, and may be accompanied by difficulty standing and ambulating, limb weakness, and deficient postural test reactions; nystagmus may also be observed. Toxicities, including carbamates, bromethalin, metaldehyde, ethylene glycol, metronidazole, theobromines, sedatives, and anticonvulsants, should be ruled out. Metabolic alterations such as hypoglycemia, hypoxia, hepatic dysfunction, hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, hypernatremia, hypokalemia, and uremia, as well as endocrine disorders such as hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, hypoadrenocorticism, and hyperadrenocorticism, can also cause neurologic dysfunction. Thiamine deficiency can result in ataxia and stupor. Traumatic injuries to the peripheral or central nervous system can also cause neurologic signs. Treatments such as calcium gluconate for hypocalcemia, atropine for organophosphate toxicity, and thiamine for thiamine deficiency should be considered based on the suspected underlying cause.

Chapter: Emergency, Toxicology, Neurology, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1742)

faqs.question: Can household toxins cause my cat to walk wobbly?
faqs.answer: Yes, many common items such as lilies, certain essential oils, human medications, and antifreeze can cause immediate neurological distress, including tremors and loss of balance.

faqs.question: What should I do if my cat is having a seizure?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat away from stairs or sharp corners to prevent injury, do not put your hands near their mouth, and transport them to an emergency vet immediately.

faqs.question: Is wobbly walking always a brain issue?
faqs.answer: Not always. While often neurological, it can also be caused by extreme weakness, inner ear infections affecting balance, or severe injuries to the limbs or spine.
